Q1: I kept things pretty simple and consistent. I'd block out time on weekday evenings after work, usually around 7 to 10 PM, and that's when I'd do most of my studying. Being 26 and working, I needed that structure or things would slip through the cracks. I'd go through the week's materials, watch videos, and try to participate in discussions when I could. It wasn't fancy, but it worked for me.
Q2: I stuck to regular times mostly. Tuesday and Thursday evenings were my main study nights, and I'd do a longer session on Sunday mornings. Deadlines would sometimes push me to add extra time, but honestly my schedule was pretty locked in because of my other responsibilities. Consistency was easier for me than trying to cram everything in at the last minute.
Q3: Usually it was just habit at a certain point. I'd get home, have dinner, and then log in to see what was new or check on discussion threads. Sometimes notifications about upcoming deadlines would prompt me, but mostly it became automatic. I'd also log in to see if anyone responded to my posts since I actually enjoyed the peer interaction aspect.
Q4: Yeah, after about the third week it just became part of my evening routine. I'd make tea, sit down at my desk, and open the LMS like it was second nature. Having that consistent schedule made it feel less like a chore and more like just what I do on those nights. The routine helped because I don't have a ton of motivation naturally, so having it be automatic took the decision-making out of it.
Q5: The video lectures were really valuable because I learn best by doing and seeing things demonstrated. I'd often pause and try things out as I watched. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. I found that the peer interactions helped concepts stick better than just reading alone.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't always complete the readings or supplementary PDFs they posted. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on screen is hard for me. I'd skim them or just rely on the video content instead. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also skipped when I felt confident enough about the material.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to the exam or assignments, I'd engage with it fully. Activities that let me actually do something rather than just read felt worth my time. I also valued anything where I could interact with classmates because that made the learning feel more real and less isolating. If something felt like busywork, I'd probably skip it.
Q8: Required activities I did no matter what, even if I wasn't feeling it. Optional ones I'd only do if I had energy left over, which honestly wasn't often. There's something about knowing something counts toward your grade that makes you push through even when motivation is low. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone and review them during my commute or lunch breaks at work. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd work through problems by hand since I'm a hands-on learner. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the computer, just me and my notes working through things manually.
Q10: My commute time was huge for studying but obviously none of that showed up anywhere. I'd listen to downloaded lectures or flip through printed notes on the bus. Also, I'd sometimes spend an hour working through practice problems in my notebook without ever logging in. The LMS would show nothing, but I was definitely putting in work.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch sections multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with slides - I'd access them once to download, then study from the downloaded version for hours. My actual engagement with the material was way more than what my login activity would suggest.
Q12: There were a few weeks where I was feeling pretty down, honestly. The sadness kind of crept in and made everything feel heavier than it needed to be. I still showed up and did the basics because that's just how I operate, but my heart wasn't fully in it. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that, but those low periods definitely affected my energy for the course.
Q13: Work was demanding sometimes, and being tired after a full day made it hard to engage deeply. When I was fatigued, I'd still log in out of habit but might just skim through things rather than really absorbing them. My low resources also meant I couldn't always access things the way I wanted - like I couldn't afford to print materials or get extra textbooks.
Q14: Yeah, there were nights I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed any of it. Those were usually the days when I was feeling sad or just mentally exhausted. I was technically there but not really present.
Q15: The assignments were tough for me to complete on time. I'd know they were important but something about starting them felt overwhelming, especially when my mood was low. I'd do well on exams because I could study in my own way, but sitting down to write assignments required a different kind of energy I didn't always have. I'd put them off until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to submit work. I'd ramp up my activity in the days before something was due. Without a deadline, I'd probably just keep reviewing material indefinitely. The structure of having due dates helped me actually finish things instead of endlessly preparing.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it encouraged me to keep going with my approach. If feedback pointed out something I missed, I'd go back and review that topic more carefully. Honestly though, I didn't always check feedback right away - sometimes I'd avoid it for a few days if I was feeling down because I didn't want to deal with potential criticism.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me a lot. Knowing exactly what was expected each week let me plan my limited study time effectively. I appreciated that things were released on a predictable schedule so I could build my routine around it. Without that structure, I probably would have struggled more to stay on track.
Q19: The video-based content worked well for my learning style. Having discussion forums where I could interact with peers also helped keep me connected. What made things harder was when there were lots of readings without video alternatives - I'd struggle to get through those. Also, some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made starting them even harder.
Q20: I think some students might be like me - they're engaging with the material in their own way but struggling with the actual submission part. Maybe anxiety about assignments, or life stuff getting in the way. You can be learning a lot and still have trouble producing the final work. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That's basically me in some ways. I did well on exams because I studied a lot offline, but my assignment completion wasn't great. If you just looked at my LMS activity, you'd miss all the time I spent with downloaded materials and my notebook. Performance can come from places the system doesn't see.
Q22: The log can show patterns - like when you're active, how often you participate in discussions, whether you're watching videos. But it completely misses offline studying, the quality of your engagement when you are online, and your mental state. It's like looking at someone's footprints and trying to understand their whole journey.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and caring about understanding it properly. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. For me, engagement happens when I'm working through problems by hand or having a real conversation with a classmate about the content. It's mental presence, not just physical presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with my mood throughout the course, or how the CBT sessions helped me keep going. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent on the bus reviewing materials, or the late nights with my notebook. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation, I built habits that carried me through. My resilience doesn't show up in login data.
